preamble: The uvular nasal, a phonetically complex and cross-linguistically rare consonant, can undergo a process of lenition where its oral occlusion is lost, leading to its vocalization into a nasalized vowel. This transformation is often observed as an allophonic variation in languages where the uvular nasal is not a stable phoneme.

examples.note: The Japanese moraic nasal /N/, which can be realized as a uvular nasal [ɴ] in certain contexts, can vocalize to a nasalized vowel, particularly in utterance-final position or before other vowels, approximants, or voiceless fricatives. The specific quality of the nasalized vowel depends on the surrounding vowels; after a back vowel like /o/, it can be realized as a nasalized close-mid back rounded vowel [õ].

sources: Trigo, R. L. (1988). The phonological derivation and behavior of nasal glides. Massachusetts Institute of Technology (Doctoral dissertation).
Kawahara, S., Sato, S. T., & Maekawa, K. (2021). Production of the utterance-final moraic nasal in Japanese: A real-time MRI study. Journal of the International Phonetic Association, 51(1), 1-27.
